content: Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is an object storage service that stores data as objects within buckets. Each bucket has a globally unique name across all AWS accounts and is created in a specific AWS Region. Objects consist of the data itself, a key (the unique identifier within the bucket), version ID, and metadata. S3 provides 99.999999999% (11 nines) of data durability by redundantly storing objects across multiple devices in multiple Availability Zones within a Region. The service is designed for 99.99% availability over a given year for the S3 Standard storage class. Access to buckets and objects is denied by default; permissions are granted explicitly through IAM policies, bucket policies, ACLs, or pre-signed URLs. S3 supports both virtual-hosted-style URLs (bucket.s3.region.amazonaws.com) and path-style URLs (s3.region.amazonaws.com/bucket), though AWS has deprecated path-style for new buckets. Common interaction patterns include the AWS CLI, SDKs in every major language, the REST API, and the S3 console. S3 is the foundation of many data lake architectures and is integrated with practically every other AWS service for data input and output.

An individual S3 object can be up to 5 TB in size. However, the largest object that can be uploaded in a single PUT request is 5 GB; objects between 100 MB and 5 TB should be uploaded using the multipart upload API, which splits the object into parts uploaded independently and assembled server-side. Multipart upload also enables parallel uploads, pause and resume, and partial retries on failure. S3 offers multiple storage classes optimized for different access patterns and cost profiles: S3 Standard for frequently accessed data, S3 Standard-IA and S3 One Zone-IA for infrequently accessed data with lower storage cost but a per-GB retrieval fee, S3 Intelligent-Tiering which automatically moves objects between access tiers based on usage, S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval for archival data needing millisecond access, S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly Glacier) with retrieval times from minutes to hours, and S3 Glacier Deep Archive for the lowest-cost long-term storage with 12-hour retrieval. Lifecycle policies can automatically transition objects between classes or expire them after a specified age. Storage class selection has substantial cost implications: Deep Archive can cost roughly 1/20th of Standard storage but with much higher retrieval latency and minimum storage durations.

S3 versioning is a bucket-level setting that, when enabled, preserves every version of every object stored in the bucket. With versioning enabled, a PUT to an existing key creates a new version with a unique version ID rather than overwriting the previous version, and a DELETE adds a delete marker that hides the object without removing prior versions. Specific versions can be retrieved by including the versionId parameter in GET requests, and the latest non-delete-marker version is returned when no version is specified. Versioning is one of the primary defenses against accidental deletion and ransomware-style data loss, and it is required to enable S3 Object Lock and certain replication features. Once versioning is enabled it cannot be disabled, only suspended, after which new objects are stored without version IDs (assigned 'null' as the version ID) but existing versions remain. Versioning increases storage costs because every version is billed; lifecycle rules can be configured to expire noncurrent versions after a defined number of days. MFA Delete is an optional protection that requires a multi-factor authentication code to permanently delete an object version or to suspend versioning, and it can only be enabled by the bucket owner using the AWS CLI or API.

S3 supports server-side encryption (SSE) for data at rest in three modes. SSE-S3 uses keys fully managed by S3 with AES-256; this is the default encryption applied to all new objects since January 2023 and requires no configuration. SSE-KMS uses AWS Key Management Service keys, allowing the customer to control the key, audit usage through CloudTrail, and apply key policies; SSE-KMS adds KMS API charges and a small latency overhead. SSE-KMS supports 'bucket keys', a feature that reduces KMS request volume by generating a short-lived bucket-scoped key, often cutting KMS costs by 99% for high-volume workloads. SSE-C lets the customer supply the encryption key in each request; S3 uses the key to encrypt or decrypt the object and then discards it, so the customer is responsible for key management and a lost key means the data is unrecoverable. Client-side encryption is also possible, where the customer encrypts data before upload using the AWS Encryption SDK or the S3 Encryption Client; in that case S3 only sees ciphertext. Bucket policies can require that all uploads include a specific encryption header (for example, x-amz-server-side-encryption: aws:kms), rejecting unencrypted PUTs.

Bucket policies are JSON resource-based policies attached directly to S3 buckets that grant or deny actions on the bucket and its objects. Unlike IAM identity-based policies, bucket policies can grant access to principals in other AWS accounts and to anonymous users, and they apply regardless of which IAM identity is calling. A bucket policy contains one or more statements with Effect (Allow or Deny), Principal (who), Action (what S3 API call), Resource (which ARN, including the bucket and/or objects with /*), and optional Conditions. Common patterns include restricting access to a specific VPC endpoint with the aws:SourceVpce condition, requiring TLS with the aws:SecureTransport condition, locking access to a CloudFront distribution by Origin Access Control (OAC) using the AWS:SourceArn condition keyed on the distribution ARN, or denying any non-encrypted upload by checking s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption. Bucket policies are evaluated together with IAM policies, ACLs, and S3 Block Public Access settings; an explicit Deny anywhere wins, and Block Public Access can override otherwise-permissive policies to prevent public exposure. The maximum size of a bucket policy is 20 KB.

S3 presigned URLs are time-limited URLs that grant temporary access to a specific object using the credentials of the principal who created the URL. They allow callers without AWS credentials, such as browsers or mobile clients, to GET, PUT, or DELETE a specific object directly against S3 without proxying through the application. A presigned URL is generated client-side by signing a canonical request using AWS Signature Version 4; no API call to AWS is required to create the URL. The URL inherits exactly the permissions of the signing principal: if the signer cannot perform the action, the presigned URL will fail when used. The expiration is configurable from 1 second to 7 days when signed with IAM user or role credentials; with temporary STS credentials, the URL is also bounded by the lifetime of those credentials, so the effective expiration is the shorter of the two. Common uses include allowing browser-based file uploads (often with a presigned POST that lets the bucket policy enforce content type and size limits), giving customers a download link to a private report, or generating short-lived URLs in a Lambda function. Because the URL embeds a valid signature, anyone who possesses it can use it until expiry; treat presigned URLs as bearer tokens.

S3 Access Points are named network endpoints attached to a bucket, each with its own hostname, IAM access point policy, and optional VPC restriction. They simplify managing access to shared buckets at scale by letting each application or team have its own access point with a tightly scoped policy, instead of cramming dozens of statements into a single bucket policy. An access point ARN includes the access point name, region, and account, and requests through the access point use a hostname like accesspoint-name-account-id.s3-accesspoint.region.amazonaws.com. Access points can be configured as 'internet' or 'VPC' origin: VPC-only access points reject any request that does not originate from the configured VPC, providing a hard network boundary. Access point policies are evaluated together with the underlying bucket policy: a request must be allowed by both the access point and the bucket. To delegate access control fully to access points, the bucket policy can be written to allow all requests that come through any access point in the same account, then the access point policies become the only effective gate. Access Points support cross-account use and are integral to S3 Object Lambda and Multi-Region Access Points.

S3 Object Lambda lets you transform the content of an object on the fly during a GET, HEAD, or LIST request without writing the transformed copy back to S3. It works by attaching a Lambda function to a special kind of access point called an Object Lambda Access Point. When a client makes a request to the Object Lambda Access Point, S3 invokes the Lambda function and passes a presigned URL for the original object plus a request context. The Lambda fetches the original object, transforms it, and returns the modified bytes via the WriteGetObjectResponse API; S3 streams that response back to the caller. Common uses include redacting PII from CSVs based on the caller's identity, converting image formats or resizing images, decrypting data with a custom KMS scheme, watermarking documents, or returning different views of the same underlying data to different audiences without storing multiple copies. Object Lambda inherits cost and latency from the Lambda invocation plus the underlying S3 GET; for large or compute-heavy transformations, latency can be substantially higher than a plain GET. The Lambda function receives the input via a presigned URL it must fetch, so it must be authorized to call s3-object-lambda:WriteGetObjectResponse and to access the source bucket through the supporting access point.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

CloudFront supports several origin types: an S3 bucket (using either the REST endpoint or the website endpoint), a custom HTTP/HTTPS origin (which can be an ALB, NLB, EC2 instance, API Gateway, MediaPackage, or any internet-reachable HTTP server), and a CloudFront origin group for failover between two origins. To restrict a private S3 bucket so it is only reachable through a specific CloudFront distribution, use Origin Access Control (OAC). OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI) and supports SigV4 signing, all S3 regions including those launched after 2022, KMS-encrypted objects, and dynamic content methods like PUT and DELETE. With OAC enabled on the origin, CloudFront signs every request to S3 with the distribution's identity, and the bucket policy can be written to allow s3:GetObject only when aws:SourceArn equals the distribution's ARN and Service is cloudfront.amazonaws.com. The bucket itself can stay private with Block Public Access on. For custom origins, CloudFront supports custom headers (sent on every origin request, often used to validate the request originated from CloudFront and not directly from the internet), origin protocol policy (HTTP only, HTTPS only, or match-viewer), and connection timeout / read timeout / keep-alive timeout settings.

CloudFront cache behaviors define how the distribution handles requests matching specific path patterns. A distribution always has a default cache behavior (path pattern *), and additional behaviors can be added with patterns like /api/*, /static/*, /*.jpg. Behaviors are matched in priority order, with the default behavior used as a fallback. Each behavior specifies the origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy (HTTP, HTTPS, redirect HTTP to HTTPS), cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, and any function or Lambda@Edge associations. Cache policies define what is included in the cache key (which headers, cookies, query strings affect caching), what TTLs apply (minimum, default, maximum), and whether to enable Gzip / Brotli compression. The TTL can also be controlled by Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin: by default CloudFront respects them, capped by the maximum TTL. Setting Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store on the origin response prevents caching at the edge. Cache invalidation is available via the CreateInvalidation API, which removes paths from the cache; the first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, additional paths cost a small per-path fee. Versioned filenames (e.g., app.v123.js) avoid the need for invalidation by changing the cache key naturally on each release.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Lambda@Edge integrates AWS Lambda with CloudFront, letting you run Node.js or Python functions in response to CloudFront events. Functions are authored and uploaded as ordinary Lambda functions in the us-east-1 region, then 'published' to a numbered version and associated with a specific cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront automatically replicates the function to all regional edge caches; replication completes within a few minutes after publishing. Functions can be associated to four event types: viewer-request, origin-request, origin-response, and viewer-response. Lambda@Edge functions execute in the closest AWS region to the viewer (not literally at every edge POP), and they have access to the full Node.js / Python runtime environment and a limited set of npm or pip packages bundled into the deployment. The maximum execution time is 5 seconds for viewer-facing events and 30 seconds for origin events; memory is configurable up to 10 GB. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second of compute, separately from regular Lambda. Common limitations: no environment variables, no VPC access, request body size limits (40 KB for viewer events, 1 MB for origin events), and a smaller set of supported runtimes than regular Lambda.

CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge are two different ways to run code at the CloudFront layer, with very different tradeoffs. CloudFront Functions are lightweight, JavaScript-only (ECMAScript 5.1 plus a subset of newer features) functions that execute in microseconds at the edge POP itself (not a regional edge cache), with a maximum execution time of about 1 ms and 2 MB of memory. They support only viewer-request and viewer-response event types, have no network or filesystem access, and cannot make outbound calls. They cost roughly 1/6 the price of Lambda@Edge per request and are designed for high-volume, low-latency request manipulation: header rewrites, simple URL normalization, redirects, A/B test cookie assignment, JWT validation against a static key, basic authorization checks. Lambda@Edge runs full Node.js or Python with up to 10 GB memory and 5–30 second timeouts, supports outbound network calls (including to other AWS services or third-party APIs), and works on origin-request and origin-response events as well as viewer events. Use CloudFront Functions for hot-path, stateless transformations and Lambda@Edge for anything requiring external lookups, larger code, or origin-side logic.

CloudFront supports geo-restriction (also called geo-blocking) at the distribution level, letting you allow or block requests based on the viewer's country as inferred from a third-party IP-to-country database. The configuration is an allowlist or blocklist of ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codes (US, GB, DE, etc.). When a viewer's request originates from a blocked country, CloudFront returns HTTP 403 before the request reaches the origin, and the response is cached so subsequent blocked requests are even cheaper. Geo-restriction is suitable for compliance-driven regional licensing (e.g., video catalog rights) but should not be relied on as a security boundary because IP-to-country mapping is imperfect and easily bypassed with a VPN. For more granular geo controls, AWS WAF can be attached to the distribution and provides geo-match rules combinable with rate limiting, IP sets, and other criteria. WAF also exposes the country in the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header (when explicitly added through a cache or origin request policy), which Lambda@Edge or origin code can use to apply business logic such as showing localized content rather than blocking outright.

CloudFront response headers policies let you add, modify, or remove HTTP response headers without involving Lambda@Edge or origin changes. A response headers policy is attached to a cache behavior and can manage four categories of headers. Security headers include Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS), Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, and X-XSS-Protection, each with managed convenience options that set sensible defaults. CORS headers include Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, Access-Control-Allow-Headers, Access-Control-Allow-Credentials, Access-Control-Expose-Headers, and Access-Control-Max-Age; CloudFront can be configured to handle preflight OPTIONS requests at the edge. Custom headers are arbitrary key-value pairs added to every response, with an option to override headers from the origin. Remove-headers strip listed headers from the origin response before sending to the viewer, useful for hiding server fingerprinting headers. AWS provides several managed response headers policies (e.g., Managed-SecurityHeadersPolicy) that can be used directly, or you can clone one to a custom policy and tune it. Using these policies avoids paying for function executions on the response path and keeps the headers consistent across cache hits and misses.

Lambda@Edge is a feature of Amazon CloudFront that runs AWS Lambda functions at CloudFront points of presence in response to viewer or origin events. Functions are written in Node.js or Python, packaged like ordinary Lambda functions, and must be deployed in the us-east-1 (N. Virginia) region. To use a function with CloudFront, you publish a numbered version (Lambda@Edge does not support $LATEST or aliases) and associate that exact version ARN with a cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront then replicates the code to all regional edge caches around the world; replication usually completes within a few minutes but can take longer. Each function executes in the AWS region nearest the viewer, not literally at the POP itself. Lambda@Edge can modify the request and response that flows between the viewer, CloudFront, and the origin; it cannot serve traffic without CloudFront. Use cases include header rewrites, A/B testing logic, JWT validation, dynamic origin selection based on geography or device, image resizing on demand, custom auth, and request normalization. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second separately from regular Lambda, and pricing is higher than standard Lambda because of the global replication infrastructure.

Lambda@Edge supports four CloudFront event types, each fired at a different point in the request lifecycle. viewer-request runs on every request from a viewer before the cache is consulted, so it sees every request and is the right place for redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should affect cache key. origin-request runs only on cache misses, just before CloudFront forwards to the origin; this is the place to rewrite the URL path or change the origin. origin-response runs after the origin returns a response and before CloudFront caches it; this is where to add or rewrite headers that should be cached, or to handle origin errors with custom responses. viewer-response runs on every response sent to the viewer, including cache hits, so it is the right place for headers that must be present on every response (though CloudFront response headers policies are usually a cheaper option). Each event type has different size limits (the viewer-side caps the request body at 40 KB, origin-side at 1 MB) and different latency budgets (viewer-side max 5 seconds, origin-side max 30 seconds). Choosing the right event is critical: putting expensive logic on viewer-request multiplies cost compared to putting it on origin-request.

Lambda@Edge functions run in a constrained execution context that differs from regular Lambda in several ways. There are no environment variables; configuration must be embedded in the function code or fetched at runtime from a service like Parameter Store. The function cannot be placed in a VPC, so it cannot reach private resources like RDS unless they are exposed through a public ingress (often an API Gateway or an internet-facing ALB). Memory is configurable but maximum execution time is capped at 5 seconds for viewer-request and viewer-response, and 30 seconds for origin-request and origin-response. The deployment package size is smaller than regular Lambda: 1 MB for viewer-side functions and 50 MB for origin-side. Layers are not supported. The function cannot use AWS X-Ray for end-to-end tracing in the usual way, though CloudWatch logs are written to the region where the function actually executed (which may be any of the supported regions globally), making centralized logging more involved. The function role must trust both lambda.amazonaws.com and edgelambda.amazonaws.com so CloudFront can invoke it during replication.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

The IAM execution role for a Lambda@Edge function must trust both lambda.amazonaws.com and edgelambda.amazonaws.com in its trust policy, because CloudFront uses edgelambda.amazonaws.com when invoking the replicated function. The role's permissions policy controls what the function can do at runtime: typically AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole for CloudWatch Logs is the minimum, plus any task-specific permissions like reading from Parameter Store, calling DynamoDB, or fetching a secret. Permissions are evaluated in whichever region the function executes, so the role must grant access globally rather than being region-scoped. Because Lambda@Edge cannot read environment variables, secrets and configuration are commonly fetched at runtime from Parameter Store or Secrets Manager and cached in the function's process memory across warm invocations to avoid per-request lookup latency. The role and function code must be designed assuming the function may run in any AWS region; cross-region calls add latency, so any data the function needs should be globally distributed (for example, in DynamoDB Global Tables or in a CloudFront-cached endpoint) rather than centralized in one region.

Common Lambda@Edge patterns include authentication, A/B testing, header rewrites, and dynamic content selection. For authentication, a viewer-request function can validate a JWT in a cookie or Authorization header against a JWKS, redirect unauthenticated users to a login page (often a Cognito Hosted UI), and forward authenticated requests to the origin with a normalized identity header. For A/B testing, a viewer-request function reads or sets a sticky cookie indicating the experiment bucket, then rewrites the request path or chooses an origin per bucket; the cookie value can also be added to the cache key so each variant is cached separately. For header rewrites, an origin-response function adds security headers (HSTS, CSP), strips sensitive headers, or normalizes Cache-Control to drive CDN behavior. For dynamic content selection, an origin-request function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header and rewrites the origin or path so users in different countries see different content while sharing a single distribution. Each pattern should be benchmarked because Lambda@Edge cost compounds quickly under high request volume; CloudFront Functions are often a cheaper alternative for simple viewer-side work.

Lambda@Edge and CloudFront Functions overlap in purpose but differ sharply in capability and cost. CloudFront Functions are JavaScript-only, single-millisecond, no-network, no-filesystem, viewer-event-only functions that run inside the CloudFront edge process at every POP; they are well suited to URL rewrites, simple header manipulation, request normalization, and JWT validation against a static key. Lambda@Edge is a full Lambda runtime in Node.js or Python with up to 10 GB memory, up to 30 seconds execution time on origin events, and full network access including AWS service calls; it is appropriate when the function needs to call DynamoDB, fetch a secret, perform image manipulation, or run on origin events. CloudFront Functions cost roughly $0.10 per million requests, while Lambda@Edge cost is request count times duration times memory, typically several times more expensive at the same request volume. The architectural rule of thumb: start with CloudFront Functions for any viewer-side transformation that fits in 1 ms and 2 MB of memory; reach for Lambda@Edge only when the function genuinely needs network calls, more compute, or to run on origin events.

Amazon Cognito provides two distinct services that are often confused: Cognito User Pools and Cognito Identity Pools. A User Pool is a managed user directory that handles sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, and federated identity, and issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) once a user authenticates. The User Pool itself is the OIDC identity provider; downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues. An Identity Pool, by contrast, exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, an external IdP like Google or Facebook, or a SAML provider) for short-lived AWS credentials via STS. Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities and are the bridge between an application's user-facing authentication and direct AWS API access from clients. A typical pattern is to authenticate users against a User Pool, get an ID token, and then exchange that token at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials that let the client (e.g., a mobile app) call S3 or DynamoDB directly. Either service can be used independently: User Pools alone for token-based API auth, Identity Pools alone for federated AWS access without a User Pool.

Cognito User Pools implement OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) standards, exposing standard endpoints like /oauth2/authorize, /oauth2/token, /oauth2/userInfo, and /.well-known/openid-configuration plus /.well-known/jwks.json. Applications register one or more app clients on the User Pool, each with its own client ID and optional client secret, allowed OAuth flows (authorization code, implicit, client credentials), allowed scopes (openid, profile, email, custom scopes), and allowed callback / logout URLs. The authorization code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for browser-based and mobile clients; the implicit flow is deprecated for interactive use. Client credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine integrations where there is no user, and requires a client secret. The token endpoint can return ID tokens (claims about the user, signed JWT), access tokens (signed JWT used for API authorization), and refresh tokens (opaque, used to obtain new access and ID tokens without re-prompting the user). All tokens are signed with RS256, and the public key is published at the JWKS endpoint so APIs can validate signatures offline.

The Cognito Hosted UI is a fully managed login, sign-up, and forgot-password page hosted by AWS at a Cognito-provided domain (auth.region.amazoncognito.com or a custom domain). It is enabled per User Pool app client and supports the OAuth 2.0 authorization endpoints; an application redirects the user to /oauth2/authorize, the user authenticates against the Hosted UI, and the user is redirected back to the configured callback URL with an authorization code or tokens. The Hosted UI handles MFA prompts, federated sign-in buttons (Google, Facebook, Apple, SAML, OIDC), password reset, and account verification flows, removing the need to build any of these screens in the application. The look and feel can be lightly customized: a logo, CSS overrides, and color tweaks. For deeper customization the only options are to fork the flow and use the Cognito SDKs directly, or to put a Lambda Pre-Sign-Up / Pre-Authentication / Pre-Token-Generation trigger between Cognito and the user. The Hosted UI is the simplest way to get a compliant OAuth flow with federation and MFA wired up, at the cost of significant customization flexibility.

After a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups) and is intended to identify the user to the application; it is not meant to authorize API calls. The access token is a JWT that contains scopes (cognito:groups, the OAuth scopes granted, and a token_use claim of 'access') and is meant to be sent on API requests for authorization. The refresh token is an opaque string used to request fresh access and ID tokens from the /oauth2/token endpoint without re-authenticating; refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked. Both ID and access tokens have a default 1-hour expiration, configurable per app client between 5 minutes and 24 hours. Both are signed with RS256 using a User Pool-specific key pair. The 'aud' claim of the ID token is the app client ID; the 'iss' claim is https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}; the 'token_use' claim is 'id' or 'access'. APIs validating tokens should always check signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and token_use.

To validate a Cognito-issued JWT, an API server fetches the JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) from the User Pool's discovery endpoint at https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json. The JWKS contains the public keys (RSA modulus and exponent) used to sign tokens, identified by 'kid' (key ID). The validation steps are: parse the JWT header to find the kid, look up the matching key in the JWKS (caching the JWKS in memory and refreshing on a kid miss), verify the RS256 signature, then validate claims: 'iss' must equal the User Pool issuer URL, 'exp' must be in the future, 'token_use' must match the expected token type (e.g., 'access' for API auth), and 'aud' (for ID tokens) or 'client_id' (for access tokens) must match an allowed app client. Cognito does not currently support full token revocation for the access and ID tokens (they are valid until expiry); only the refresh token can be revoked, which prevents new access tokens from being issued. Many libraries (aws-jwt-verify, jose, python-jose, PyJWT) implement these checks; rolling your own validator is an easy place to introduce auth bugs.

By default, a Cognito User Pool exposes its OAuth endpoints under a Cognito-owned domain like https://{prefix}.auth.{region}.amazoncognito.com, where {prefix} is a unique subdomain claimed when the User Pool's domain is set up. For production applications, a custom domain such as auth.example.com is usually preferred for branding and trust. To use a custom domain, you provide an ACM certificate that must be in us-east-1 regardless of where the User Pool is, and you must own the domain (verified via DNS). The Cognito service then provisions a CloudFront distribution behind the scenes and returns its alias target; you create a DNS A or CNAME record (depending on whether you want to use Route 53 alias) pointing your custom domain to that target. The custom domain serves the same Hosted UI and OAuth endpoints as the default domain, just under your branded host. The parent domain (example.com) must already have an A record before you can create the auth.example.com Cognito custom domain. Custom domain changes can take up to an hour to propagate due to the underlying CloudFront update.

Cognito User Pools support federation with external identity providers in two main flavors. Social federation supports Google, Facebook, Apple, and Amazon out of the box: you register an OAuth app with the provider, add the client ID and secret to the User Pool's identity provider configuration, and choose attribute mappings from the provider's claims to User Pool attributes. Once configured, the Hosted UI displays a sign-in-with-{provider} button, and on first login Cognito creates a federated user in the User Pool linked to the external identity. SAML federation supports any SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Auth0, etc.): you upload the IdP metadata XML or URL, configure attribute mappings, and Cognito acts as the SAML SP. OIDC federation supports any OIDC-compliant IdP: you supply the issuer URL, client ID, secret, and authorized scopes. Federated users are created lazily on first sign-in and have a 'sub' that is unique within the User Pool; the original IdP identity is recorded in the user's identities attribute. Multiple IdPs can be enabled simultaneously; the Hosted UI shows one button per enabled provider plus the standard username/password form if local sign-in is allowed.

Cognito User Pools support multi-factor authentication (MFA) at the User Pool level and per app client. MFA can be set to OFF (no MFA prompted), ON (every user must enroll and complete MFA on every sign-in), or OPTIONAL (users may enroll and, if they do, will be prompted on sign-in). Two factor types are supported: SMS (a one-time code sent to a verified phone number, requires SNS configured for the User Pool and an spending limit raise from AWS for production volumes) and TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password compatible with Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, and other authenticator apps). TOTP is recommended over SMS due to SMS-interception risk and SNS cost. The Hosted UI handles MFA enrollment and prompts automatically, including showing a QR code for TOTP setup. For application-side flows using the Cognito SDKs directly, the InitiateAuth response will return a 'SOFTWARE_TOKEN_MFA' or 'SMS_MFA' challenge that the app must complete before tokens are issued. Adaptive authentication is an additional Cognito feature that uses risk signals (new device, new location, suspicious IP) to demand MFA only on risky sign-ins, even with MFA set to OPTIONAL.

AWS IAM (Identity and Access Management) is the foundation for authentication and authorization across AWS services. Three core principal types exist. An IAM user is a long-lived identity with a name, optional console password, and optional access key pair (access key ID + secret access key); users represent humans or service accounts that need their own credentials. A role is an identity with a permissions policy that no one owns directly; roles are assumed by trusted principals (users, services, federated identities) via the STS AssumeRole API, returning short-lived temporary credentials. A group is a collection of users to which permissions policies can be attached for easier management; users in the group inherit the group's policies but groups themselves cannot be principals (they cannot make API calls). Best practice is to avoid IAM users where possible: use IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) for human access and use roles for application-to-AWS access via instance profiles, EKS service accounts, Lambda execution roles, and service-linked roles. Long-lived access keys are a common breach vector; short-lived credentials from roles dramatically reduce blast radius.

IAM has two main policy categories: identity-based and resource-based. Identity-based policies are attached to a user, group, or role and define what actions that identity can perform on which resources. They take three sub-forms: AWS managed policies (maintained by AWS, identified by an arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/ ARN), customer managed policies (created and maintained by the customer, reusable across many identities), and inline policies (embedded directly in a single identity, not reusable). Resource-based policies are attached directly to a resource and define which principals can do what on that resource; they are supported by S3 buckets, KMS keys, SNS topics, SQS queues, Lambda functions, Secrets Manager secrets, and a handful of other services. Resource-based policies can grant cross-account access without needing role assumption (the resource policy names the external principal directly). When both an identity-based policy and a resource-based policy apply, the union of their permissions applies for same-account access, and explicit cross-account access requires both sides to allow it. A third category, permission boundaries, sets the maximum permissions a role or user can have, regardless of what their identity-based policies grant.

IAM policy evaluation logic determines whether a request is allowed or denied by combining all applicable policies. The evaluation flow: start with an implicit deny, evaluate all applicable policies (identity-based, resource-based, permission boundaries, Service Control Policies from AWS Organizations, session policies on assumed roles), and apply the rule that an explicit Deny in any policy always overrides any Allow. If no Deny matches, an Allow in at least one policy is required for the request to succeed. Specifically: a Service Control Policy (SCP) from AWS Organizations sets the outer boundary for the account; nothing outside an SCP-allowed action is permitted no matter what other policies say. Permission boundaries similarly cap a single principal's effective permissions. Session policies passed to AssumeRole or GetFederationToken further narrow what a session can do without granting new permissions. For cross-account requests both the trusting account's resource policy (or a permission allowing AssumeRole) and the trusted account's identity policy must allow the action. The single most useful rule of thumb: when in doubt, an explicit Deny wins over any Allow.

An IAM role's trust policy is a special resource-based policy on the role itself that defines which principals are allowed to assume the role via STS AssumeRole, AssumeRoleWithSAML, or AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity. The trust policy specifies a Principal (which can be an AWS account, an IAM user, another role, an AWS service like ec2.amazonaws.com or lambda.amazonaws.com, a SAML provider, or a federated identity provider), an Action (sts:AssumeRole, sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity, sts:AssumeRoleWithSAML, or sts:TagSession), and optional Conditions (sts:ExternalId for cross-account vendor access, aws:PrincipalTag, aws:SourceIp, MFA presence, and many others). When a principal calls AssumeRole, STS verifies that the trust policy allows the call AND that the calling principal has sts:AssumeRole permission on the role; both must be satisfied. Successful assumption returns a set of temporary credentials (access key, secret key, session token) valid for 15 minutes to 12 hours, with a default of 1 hour. The session inherits the role's permissions policies, optionally narrowed by a session policy passed in the AssumeRole call. ExternalId is the recommended pattern for vendor-to-customer cross-account access to defend against the confused-deputy problem.

AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) is AWS's recommended way to manage human access across multiple AWS accounts. It provides a single sign-on portal where users authenticate once and then choose an account and a permission set; behind the scenes, IAM Identity Center provisions IAM roles in each target account that match the permission sets and brokers temporary credentials via AssumeRole. Permission sets are reusable bundles of identity-based policies (AWS managed policies, customer managed policies referenced by name, or inline policies); assigning a permission set to a user/group + account combination causes Identity Center to create or update the corresponding IAM role in that account. Users and groups can come from the built-in Identity Center store, an external IdP via SAML/OIDC (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace), or AWS Directory Service. Sessions issued through Identity Center are short-lived (configurable up to 12 hours) and revocable. Identity Center replaces the older pattern of one IAM user per person per account, dramatically reducing the number of long-lived credentials and providing a centralized audit trail. The aws sso login CLI command and the AWS Toolkit IDE extensions both natively support Identity Center.

Service-linked roles are IAM roles that are predefined by an AWS service and linked to that service. They have a specific naming pattern (AWSServiceRoleFor{ServiceName}) and a trust policy that allows only the owning service to assume them. The permissions are managed by AWS via an AWS managed policy (often named AWSServiceRolePolicy{ServiceName}), and the customer typically cannot edit the role's policies. Service-linked roles exist because some AWS services need to call other AWS services on your behalf in the background (e.g., Amazon ECS calls EC2 to manage container instances, Auto Scaling calls EC2 to launch instances, Elastic Load Balancing calls EC2 and CloudWatch). They are usually created automatically the first time you use the service, but can be created manually with iam:CreateServiceLinkedRole. They cannot be deleted while the linked service still has resources that depend on them; deletion is possible only after the dependencies are removed and the service confirms the role is no longer needed. Service-linked roles simplify permission management for service-to-service integration and reduce the chance of an operator accidentally removing a permission that breaks the service.

An EC2 instance profile is a container for an IAM role that an EC2 instance can use. Despite often being conflated with the role itself, the instance profile is a distinct IAM resource: it has an ARN, a name, and contains exactly one role. When an EC2 instance is launched with an instance profile attached, the AWS metadata service on the instance (the IMDS at 169.254.169.254) makes the role's temporary credentials available to any process on the instance, automatically rotated before expiration. The AWS SDKs and CLI discover credentials from IMDS by default. IMDSv2 (token-based, more secure against SSRF attacks) should be enforced because IMDSv1 (no token) is reachable by any process that can make HTTP requests, including a vulnerable web server tricked into making outbound calls. When you use the console to attach a role to an EC2 instance, AWS creates the instance profile for you under the hood with the same name as the role, which is why the distinction is often invisible. Programmatically (CloudFormation, Terraform), the role and instance profile must usually be created as separate resources. ECS tasks, EKS pods (via IRSA), and Lambda use their own role-attachment mechanisms and do not use instance profiles.

IAM policy Conditions narrow when a statement applies, supporting a wide library of condition operators (StringEquals, StringLike, NumericLessThan, DateGreaterThan, IpAddress, ArnLike, Bool, Null) and condition keys (aws:SourceIp, aws:PrincipalTag, aws:RequestTag, aws:ResourceTag, aws:CurrentTime, aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent, aws:RequestedRegion, aws:SourceVpc, aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalOrgID, aws:SecureTransport, and service-specific keys like s3:prefix, kms:EncryptionContext, ec2:InstanceType). Conditions enable powerful patterns: requiring MFA, restricting access to a specific VPC endpoint, allowing actions only in certain regions, applying ABAC by matching principal tags to resource tags. Policy variables let you parameterize a single policy with values from the request context: ${aws:username}, ${aws:PrincipalTag/Department}, ${aws:userid}, ${saml:sub} can appear inside Resource ARNs or Condition values and are substituted at evaluation time. A common pattern is to grant a single S3 statement that resolves to s3:GetObject on arn:aws:s3:::company-bucket/${aws:username}/* so each user gets a personal prefix without writing per-user policies. Policy variables and conditions together form the basis for scalable, attribute-based access control.

An Application Load Balancer (ALB) is a layer 7 load balancer in the AWS Elastic Load Balancing family, designed for HTTP and HTTPS traffic. It terminates TLS, parses requests at the application layer, and routes them to backend targets based on rules that consider the request path, host header, HTTP method, query parameters, source IP, and HTTP headers. An ALB consists of a load balancer (the deployed entity with one or more public or internal IPs in each Availability Zone), one or more listeners (each on a TCP port like 80 or 443), and one or more target groups (the actual backends). The ALB integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free TLS certificates, supports HTTP/2 and gRPC, supports WebSocket upgrades, and can authenticate users via Cognito or any OIDC IdP before forwarding the request. ALBs scale automatically with traffic and are billed by the hour plus by Load Balancer Capacity Units (LCUs), a composite metric covering new connections, active connections, processed bytes, and rule evaluations. ALBs are the right choice for HTTP/HTTPS workloads needing path-based or host-based routing; for raw TCP/UDP/TLS-passthrough at scale, Network Load Balancer (NLB) is the alternative.

ALB listeners hold one or more rules that decide where each request goes. The default rule applies when no other rule matches, and additional rules are evaluated in priority order (lowest priority number first). Each rule has one or more conditions and one or more actions. Conditions can match host header, path pattern, HTTP request method, query string, source IP CIDR, or HTTP header value, and multiple conditions on a rule are ANDed together. Actions can forward to a target group (with weighted forwarding for blue/green or canary deployments), redirect to a different URL (301 or 302), return a fixed response (e.g., 200 OK with a body, useful for health checks or maintenance pages), authenticate using Cognito or generic OIDC and then forward, or invoke a Lambda function directly. Up to 100 rules per listener are supported by default. A typical rule set might route /api/* to one target group, /admin/* to another (with a Cognito authenticate action in front), and the default to a static-site target group. Rules can be reorganized by changing priority without downtime.

ALB target groups define the actual backends that receive traffic. There are three target types. Instance target groups register EC2 instances by instance ID; the ALB sends traffic to the primary private IP of each instance on the configured port. IP target groups register IP addresses directly; this supports targets in peered VPCs, on-premises servers reachable over Direct Connect or VPN, and ECS tasks running with the awsvpc network mode. Lambda target groups invoke a Lambda function for each request, with the request payload converted to a Lambda event and the function's response mapped back to an HTTP response; this enables serverless backends behind ALB with all the listener rule features and Cognito authentication. Each target group has its own protocol (HTTP or HTTPS), port, and health check configuration. A target can register against multiple target groups, useful for sharing a backend across distributions or for blue/green setups. The ALB performs health checks against each target on a configurable path and considers a target healthy after 'HealthyThresholdCount' consecutive successes; targets failing 'UnhealthyThresholdCount' consecutive checks are marked unhealthy and stop receiving traffic.

ALB health checks continuously probe each target to determine whether it is healthy and should receive traffic. Health check parameters include the protocol (HTTP or HTTPS), port (the same as the target group's traffic port or an override port), path (default '/'), success codes (default '200', can be a range or comma-separated list like '200-299' or '200,202'), interval in seconds (default 30), timeout in seconds (default 5, must be less than interval), HealthyThresholdCount (default 5 consecutive successes to mark healthy), and UnhealthyThresholdCount (default 2 consecutive failures to mark unhealthy). A target starts in 'initial' state, becomes 'healthy' after passing HealthyThresholdCount checks, and may move to 'unhealthy' on failures or 'draining' during deregistration. While 'draining', the target finishes in-flight requests but receives no new ones, controlled by the deregistration delay (default 300 seconds). Slow-starting targets can use the slow start mode, which ramps up traffic gradually over a configurable window once a target is marked healthy, useful for warm-up of caches or JIT-compiled languages. Misconfigured health checks (wrong path, wrong port, too aggressive interval) are a common cause of repeated target flapping.

ALB sticky sessions ensure that a given client is consistently routed to the same target for the duration of the session, useful when the application maintains in-memory state per user. Two stickiness modes are supported per target group. Application-based stickiness uses an application-supplied cookie (the application sets the cookie name and value, the ALB reads it on subsequent requests). Duration-based stickiness uses a cookie generated and managed by the ALB itself, named AWSALB by default, set with a configurable lifetime from 1 second to 7 days. While stickiness improves session affinity it conflicts with horizontal scaling: a target's failure invalidates all sessions pinned to it, and uneven session distribution can lead to one target receiving the majority of traffic. Modern application architectures usually externalize session state to ElastiCache, DynamoDB, or a JWT in a cookie so that any target can serve any request, removing the need for stickiness. Stickiness is set at the target group level and applies to all listener rules forwarding to that group; it can be combined with weighted target groups for blue/green deployments, but mid-deployment cookie behavior must be understood to avoid pinning users to the wrong version.

ALB supports mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication on HTTPS listeners, allowing clients to authenticate with a client certificate in addition to the server certificate. Two modes are available. In passthrough mode, the ALB forwards the client certificate as a base64-encoded value in the X-Amzn-Mtls-Clientcert header to the target without performing any validation; the backend application is responsible for validating the certificate. In verify mode, the ALB validates the client certificate against a trust store you upload to the load balancer (a PEM bundle of trusted root and intermediate CAs stored in S3), and only forwards the request if validation succeeds; details of the validated certificate are added as headers like X-Amzn-Mtls-Clientcert-Subject, X-Amzn-Mtls-Clientcert-Issuer, X-Amzn-Mtls-Clientcert-Serial-Number, and X-Amzn-Mtls-Clientcert-Validity. Trust stores can include up to 10 certificate revocation lists (CRLs). mTLS is commonly used for service-to-service authentication between trusted partners, for IoT device authentication, or for zero-trust internal architectures. mTLS only works on dedicated TLS listeners, not on the default HTTP listener; certificate negotiation also adds a small latency overhead to TLS handshake.

ALB integrates with AWS WAF for layer 7 filtering and with S3 for access logging. AWS WAF can be associated with an ALB to apply web ACLs containing managed rule groups (e.g., AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet, AWSManagedRulesKnownBadInputsRuleSet, AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet) and custom rules (rate limits, IP allow/blocklists, geo-match, regex, size constraints). WAF evaluates rules before the request reaches the ALB targets and can block, count, or CAPTCHA-challenge offending requests; metrics are reported per rule for visibility. ALB access logs, when enabled, are written to a configured S3 bucket every 5 minutes (with one file per node per AZ). The log format is space-separated text with fields including timestamp, ALB name, client IP and port, target IP and port, processing times for request/target/response, ALB status code, target status code, sent and received bytes, request line, user agent, SSL cipher and protocol, target group ARN, trace ID (for X-Ray), domain name, certificate ARN, matched rule priority, request creation time, action executed, redirect URL, error reason, and TLS version. Access logs do not include request or response bodies, only headers and metadata.

Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's container orchestration service. The top-level resource is a cluster, a logical grouping in which tasks and services run. A cluster does not by itself reserve any compute; it can host tasks that run on EC2 instances registered to the cluster (EC2 launch type) or on the AWS-managed Fargate compute engine (Fargate launch type, no instance management required). Tasks are the unit of execution: a task is one or more co-scheduled containers defined by a task definition. ECS schedules tasks onto available capacity according to placement strategies (binpack, random, spread by attribute) and constraints (instance type, AMI, custom attributes). ECS integrates with IAM for task and execution roles, with VPC for networking, with CloudWatch for logs and metrics, with ECR for image storage, and with Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer for ingress. ECS clusters are free; you pay only for the compute (EC2 instance hours or Fargate vCPU/memory seconds) plus any associated data transfer and load balancer charges. ECS competes with EKS (Kubernetes) and is generally simpler to operate, with the tradeoff of being AWS-specific.

An ECS task definition is a JSON document that describes how to run one or more containers as a unit. Top-level fields include family (a name plus auto-incremented revision), task role ARN (the IAM role the containers can assume for AWS API calls), execution role ARN (the IAM role ECS itself uses to pull images and write logs), network mode, requiresCompatibilities (EC2, FARGATE, or both), cpu and memory at the task level (required for Fargate, optional for EC2), and a list of containerDefinitions. Each container definition specifies image, name, cpu, memory or memoryReservation, essential flag (if true, failure of this container fails the entire task), portMappings, environment variables, secrets (resolved from Secrets Manager or Parameter Store), mountPoints, dependsOn (container ordering), healthCheck, logConfiguration (typically the awslogs driver writing to CloudWatch), and ulimits. Task definitions are immutable per revision; updating means registering a new revision and pointing services at it. The maximum task definition size is 64 KB. Task definitions can include sidecar containers for logging, service mesh proxies, or init steps that run before the main container.

ECS supports two ways to launch tasks: standalone tasks via RunTask, and long-running services. RunTask launches a single instance of a task definition that runs to completion (or until stopped) and is appropriate for batch jobs, scheduled tasks via EventBridge, or one-off operations. A service is a controller that maintains a desired count of running tasks, replaces failed tasks, performs rolling deployments when the task definition is updated, and registers/deregisters tasks with one or more target groups behind an ALB or NLB. Service deployments support rolling updates (the default, controlled by minimumHealthyPercent and maximumPercent), blue/green via CodeDeploy (a separate green task set is created and traffic is shifted on success), and external deployment controllers. Services can use ECS Service Discovery (Cloud Map) to register tasks under DNS names, capacity providers to spread tasks across multiple compute strategies (e.g., Fargate + Fargate Spot), and auto-scaling policies based on CloudWatch metrics or target tracking. Services have circuit breakers that automatically roll back a deployment if too many tasks fail health checks consecutively. Choose RunTask for ephemeral work and services for anything that must keep running.

ECS supports two launch types that differ fundamentally in compute responsibility. The EC2 launch type runs tasks on EC2 instances that you manage as part of the cluster: you choose instance types, capacity, autoscaling rules, AMI (typically the ECS-optimized Amazon Linux 2023 AMI), patch cadence, and SSH access. The ECS agent on each instance registers it with the cluster and accepts task placements. EC2 launch type is more cost-effective at scale (especially with Reserved Instances or Spot), supports daemons that must run on every host, and allows custom AMIs for specific kernel modules or tooling, but requires capacity planning and OS patching. The Fargate launch type abstracts away EC2 entirely: you specify CPU and memory at the task level, ECS provisions the right amount of compute, and you pay per vCPU-second and GB-second the task runs. Fargate has no host to patch, automatically isolates each task at the kernel level (each task gets its own dedicated VM), and scales to zero when no tasks are running. Tradeoffs: Fargate has limited customization, generally costs more per vCPU-hour than equivalent EC2, has longer task start times (commonly 30–60 seconds versus a few seconds on a warmed EC2 instance), and supports a subset of network and storage features.

ECS task definitions specify a network mode that controls how containers communicate. The 'awsvpc' mode (required for Fargate, recommended for EC2) gives each task its own elastic network interface (ENI) with a private IP in your VPC and its own security group; containers in the task share the network namespace and reach each other via localhost. This is the most isolated mode and the only one that integrates cleanly with ALB IP target groups. The 'bridge' mode (EC2 only) uses Docker's default bridge network on the host; containers get IPs on a private docker0 bridge and reach the outside world via host NAT, with port mappings exposing container ports on host ports (which can be static or 'host: 0' for dynamic). The 'host' mode (EC2 only) shares the host's network namespace; containers bind directly to host ports and there is no isolation, providing the highest performance but the worst security and limiting one container per port per host. The 'none' mode disables external networking entirely, useful for jobs that need only the loopback interface. Modern deployments overwhelmingly use awsvpc for the per-task ENI security boundary and ALB compatibility.

ECS Service Discovery integrates with AWS Cloud Map to publish DNS records for tasks in a service so other services can discover them by hostname. When you enable service discovery on an ECS service, ECS registers each task in a Cloud Map service that is part of a private namespace (Route 53 private hosted zone) or a public namespace. Two record types are supported: A records (for awsvpc mode tasks, mapping the service hostname to each task's ENI private IP) and SRV records (which include port info, useful for bridge or host mode tasks where multiple tasks may share a host with different ports). When tasks are added or removed, ECS updates Cloud Map automatically. Other services can resolve service.namespace and get DNS-load-balanced answers across the registered tasks. Service Discovery is a lightweight alternative to a service mesh and a load balancer for east-west traffic where the client is happy to do DNS-level load balancing. For more sophisticated traffic management (retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability), AWS App Mesh or service mesh tools layer on top. Pricing covers Cloud Map API calls and Route 53 DNS queries; both are inexpensive for typical service counts.

ECS Exec lets you run an interactive command (typically a shell) inside a running container, similar to 'docker exec' but for ECS tasks. It works on both Fargate and EC2 launch types and uses AWS Systems Manager Session Manager under the hood: the SSM agent runs as a sidecar process inside the container, opening an outbound connection to SSM, and the user's CLI command opens a session through that channel. Prerequisites: enableExecuteCommand must be true on the service or RunTask call, the task role must include the ssmmessages permissions (CreateControlChannel, CreateDataChannel, OpenControlChannel, OpenDataChannel), the container image must include /bin/sh or another shell, and (for Fargate) the platform version must be 1.4.0 or later. The aws ecs execute-command CLI command takes the cluster, task ID, container name, command, and an --interactive flag. ECS Exec is invaluable for debugging stuck or misbehaving production tasks without rebuilding images; sessions are logged to CloudWatch Logs and/or S3 for audit. Execution should be limited to break-glass scenarios; the ability to exec into a container is essentially full code execution as the container user, so the IAM permission to call execute-command should be tightly scoped.

AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that runs ECS tasks and EKS pods without managing EC2 instances. With Fargate, you specify the CPU and memory each task or pod needs, and AWS provisions the underlying infrastructure transparently, charging per second of resource usage. Compared with the EC2 launch type for ECS, Fargate removes capacity planning, AMI management, OS patching, and cluster scaling: there is no host to log into, no Docker daemon to manage, no instance autoscaling group to tune. Each Fargate task runs in its own isolated VM rather than sharing a kernel with other tasks (this is implemented with Firecracker microVMs), which provides a stronger isolation boundary than container-level isolation on a shared host. The tradeoffs are higher per-vCPU cost than equivalent EC2, longer task start times (commonly 30–60 seconds), no daemon containers, no GPU support (as of typical AWS regions; some regions are gaining GPU Fargate availability), and a smaller set of supported features (no privileged containers, no host networking, no Windows Server 2016, limited storage options). Fargate is the default choice for sporadic, unpredictable, or low-volume workloads where operational simplicity outweighs per-hour cost.

Fargate task sizing is constrained to a fixed grid of CPU and memory combinations, not arbitrary values. Valid CPU sizes are 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 vCPU. For each CPU size, only certain memory values are supported: 0.25 vCPU supports 0.5–2 GB in 0.5 GB increments; 0.5 vCPU supports 1–4 GB in 1 GB increments; 1 vCPU supports 2–8 GB; 2 vCPU supports 4–16 GB; 4 vCPU supports 8–30 GB; 8 vCPU supports 16–60 GB in 4 GB increments; 16 vCPU supports 32–120 GB in 8 GB increments. Specifying an unsupported combination results in an InvalidParameterException at task launch. Memory and CPU are both at the task level; container-level limits within the task share the task's pool. Right-sizing is important: oversizing wastes money (you pay for what you ask for, not what you use), undersizing can OOM-kill containers or throttle CPU. CloudWatch Container Insights and the per-task metrics from the awsvpc trunking ENI help right-size after observing real usage; many production deployments end up at 0.5–1 vCPU for typical web workloads.

Fargate platform versions are versions of the Fargate runtime environment that determine which features and behaviors are available. Each platform version is identified by a number like 1.3.0 or 1.4.0; AWS publishes new versions periodically and old versions are eventually deprecated. Setting platformVersion to 'LATEST' on a service or RunTask call means the most recent version supported in that region; pinning to a specific version (e.g., '1.4.0') gives reproducibility but requires the operator to keep up with AWS deprecation announcements. Platform versions also gate feature availability: ephemeral storage above 20 GB requires 1.4.0+, ECS Exec requires 1.4.0+, and various networking and IAM enhancements have shipped in different versions. When a service's platform version is updated to LATEST, existing tasks continue running on their original version until the next deployment replaces them; a service deployment is the only way to roll tasks onto a new platform version. Platform versions are independent of the ECS agent and Docker versions on EC2; they apply only to Fargate. Production services typically pin to a specific version and bump deliberately during a deployment window rather than tracking LATEST.

Fargate ephemeral storage is a temporary, container-writable disk attached to each task. By default, every Fargate task gets 20 GB of free ephemeral storage shared among the containers in the task, mounted at the container's writable layer. Tasks can request additional ephemeral storage up to 200 GB by setting ephemeralStorage.sizeInGiB on the task definition; the additional storage above 20 GB is billed per GB per hour. Ephemeral storage is exactly that: ephemeral. It is destroyed when the task stops, and there is no way to recover its contents. For persistent storage, Fargate supports mounting Amazon EFS file systems via the volumes block in the task definition; EFS provides shared, durable storage across tasks. As of platform version 1.4.0, EBS volumes can also be attached to Fargate tasks for higher-performance per-task block storage; this is a newer capability and supports gp3 volumes. The most common storage pattern is to write logs and temp files to ephemeral storage (relying on awslogs to ship logs out before they are lost) and use S3 or DynamoDB for application state, avoiding the need to attach external block storage at all.

Each Fargate task gets its own elastic network interface (ENI) attached when it starts, with a private IP in the configured subnet and the task's security group. This per-task ENI is the underlying mechanism that makes Fargate's awsvpc-only network model work: every task is, from the VPC's perspective, indistinguishable from an EC2 instance with one ENI. Because ENIs are a finite per-subnet resource and each task consumes one IP, large Fargate fleets in small subnets can exhaust IPs and fail to launch new tasks. Right-sizing subnet CIDRs is important: a /24 subnet (256 addresses minus AWS reservations) supports at most a few hundred concurrent Fargate tasks; production deployments often use /20 or larger. ENI provisioning adds 5–20 seconds to task start time historically; AWS introduced ENI trunking on supported instance types (and similar amortization on Fargate) to reduce this overhead. Outbound network access from a Fargate task follows VPC routing rules: tasks in private subnets need a NAT gateway, VPC endpoint, or transit gateway path to reach AWS APIs and the public internet. Tasks pulling images from ECR over the public endpoint need NAT or an ECR VPC endpoint; using interface endpoints for ECR, S3 (gateway endpoint), CloudWatch Logs, and SSM is a common production pattern.

Fargate pricing is based on the vCPU and memory you request for each task, billed per second from when the task starts pulling its image until it stops, with a one-minute minimum. The published rates differ by region and by capacity flavor: standard Fargate, Fargate Spot (significantly cheaper but tasks can be interrupted with 2 minutes of warning when AWS reclaims the capacity), and Compute Savings Plans (a commitment-based discount applicable to Fargate, EC2, and Lambda compute). Storage above the included 20 GB ephemeral is billed per GB-hour, EFS access is billed per GB-month and per access (with throughput options), and data transfer follows VPC pricing. Compared with EC2 launch type, Fargate's per-vCPU rate is roughly 20–50% higher, but Fargate has zero idle cost when tasks are not running, no overhead for scaling reserve, and no instance management labor. The break-even between Fargate and EC2 depends on utilization: highly utilized, steady workloads tend to be cheaper on EC2 (especially with Reserved Instances or Savings Plans on EC2), while bursty or low-utilization workloads are cheaper on Fargate. Fargate Spot can dramatically lower cost for batch and fault-tolerant workloads where interruption is acceptable.

Fargate task start time is dominated by image pull, ENI provisioning, and platform-version-specific overheads, typically running 30–60 seconds end to end for a moderately sized image, and longer for large images or images pulled across regions. Compared with starting a container on a warmed EC2 instance (which can take a few seconds because the host kernel and Docker daemon are already running), Fargate has a meaningful cold-start penalty because each task is launched onto a freshly provisioned microVM. Image pull dominates start time when images are large; using slim base images (Alpine, distroless), multi-stage builds, and pulling from ECR in the same region (or via an interface VPC endpoint) all reduce pull time materially. Once a task is running, performance is comparable to a similarly sized EC2 instance because the underlying microVM uses the same hardware. There is no per-request cold start as in Lambda; the cold start cost is paid once per task, not per request. To mitigate task startup latency for serving traffic, services typically maintain a healthy minimum task count and scale ahead of demand using target tracking on CPU or request count, rather than relying on scaling reactively when latency spikes.
